nodes.question: What area concerns you most right now?
Do you have a headache?
Is this the worst headache of your life, or did it start suddenly like a thunderclap?

nodes.diagnosisBody: A sudden severe thunderclap headache is a red flag for subarachnoid hemorrhage. Call 911 immediately. Do not wait.

nodes.question: Do you have a stiff neck, fever, or sensitivity to bright light?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Headache with neck stiffness, fever, and photophobia is the classic triad of meningitis. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate evaluation. Go to the emergency department now.

nodes.question: Does the headache throb on one side with nausea, visual changes, or sensitivity to sound?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Unilateral throbbing headache with nausea and photo/phonophobia is consistent with migraine. Treatment: triptans for acute episodes, preventive therapy if >4/month. Avoid triggers. Consult a neurologist if frequent.

nodes.question: Does the headache feel like a tight band around your head on both sides?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Bilateral band-like pressure is characteristic of tension headache — the most common primary headache. Responds to paracetamol 500-1000mg or ibuprofen 400mg. Address stress, posture, screen time.

nodes.question: Do you have visual disturbances, one-sided weakness, or difficulty speaking?
Do you have dizziness, vertigo, or balance problems?
Does the room spin when you change head position (turning in bed, looking up)?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Brief vertigo triggered by head position changes is characteristic of BPPV. Treatment: the Epley maneuver is effective in ~80% of cases. Consult ENT if symptoms persist >2 weeks.

nodes.question: Do you have ringing in your ears and/or hearing loss along with the vertigo?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Episodic vertigo with fluctuating hearing loss and tinnitus suggests Ménière's disease. Episodes last 20min to hours. Low-sodium diet, betahistine, audiometry and MRI recommended. Refer to ENT.

Dizziness without positional triggers or hearing changes may indicate vestibular neuritis, orthostatic hypotension, or medication side effects. Check BP lying/standing, blood tests (CBC, glucose, thyroid). Consult your doctor.

nodes.question: Do you have vision changes (blurry, double, or loss)?
Was the vision loss sudden and affects one eye?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Sudden painless monocular vision loss may indicate central retinal artery occlusion — the ophthalmologic equivalent of a stroke. Call 911 immediately.
Gradual vision changes can indicate refractive error, cataracts, glaucoma, or diabetic retinopathy. Schedule an ophthalmologist appointment for comprehensive exam.

Sudden visual disturbance, unilateral weakness, or speech difficulty are hallmarks of stroke. Remember FAST: Face, Arm, Speech, Time. Call 911 immediately. Even if symptoms resolve (TIA), urgent evaluation is critical.
Your neurological symptoms don't match a clear pattern. Consult a physician for neurological examination, consider CT/MRI if symptoms are new or worsening.

nodes.question: Do you have chest pain or tightness?
Does the pain feel like pressure or squeezing, radiating to your left arm, jaw, neck, or back?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Crushing chest pain radiating to arm/jaw/back is the classic presentation of myocardial infarction. Call 911 immediately. Chew aspirin 325mg while waiting. Do NOT drive yourself.

nodes.question: Does the pain worsen with deep breathing, coughing, or position changes?
Do you also have fever or recent viral illness?
Do you have heartburn or acid taste, worsening after eating?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Sharp chest pain worsening with inspiration, improving sitting forward, after viral illness suggests pericarditis. Treatment: NSAIDs + colchicine. Echo to rule out effusion. Seek evaluation today.
Pleuritic chest pain suggests pleural inflammation (needs CXR) or costochondritis (reproducible on palpation, benign). Responds to NSAIDs.

Burning substernal pain with acid taste and postprandial worsening suggests GERD. Start PPI (omeprazole 20mg daily) for 4-8 weeks. Lifestyle: avoid late meals, elevate bed head. Endoscopy if no improvement.
Non-specific chest pain should be evaluated to rule out cardiac causes. Recommended: ECG, troponin, possibly stress test. See a cardiologist, especially with risk factors.

nodes.question: Do you have a cough?
Has the cough lasted more than 3 weeks?
Are you coughing up blood?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Coughing up blood requires urgent evaluation. CXR, CT chest, CBC, coagulation, sputum cultures (including AFB for TB). If volume is large or you feel faint, call 911.
Cough >3 weeks: top 3 causes in non-smokers are post-nasal drip, asthma, GERD. In smokers, exclude COPD and lung cancer. CXR, spirometry, and empiric treatment trials recommended.

nodes.question: Do you have fever and colored (yellow/green) sputum?
Do you have difficulty breathing at rest?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Acute cough without fever or purulent sputum is most likely viral (common cold). Duration 7-14 days. Supportive care: rest, hydration, honey. Antibiotics NOT indicated. See doctor if worsening after day 7.
Productive cough with fever and dyspnea suggests pneumonia. Requires CXR. Treatment: amoxicillin 1g TID or azithromycin. If severely ill (confusion, low O2), go to emergency department.
Productive cough with mild fever, no dyspnea at rest — acute bronchitis, usually viral. Resolves 1-3 weeks. Antibiotics generally NOT needed. See doctor if fever >38.5°C persists >3 days.

nodes.question: Do you have shortness of breath?
Did it start suddenly (minutes to hours)?
Do you have calf pain/swelling, or recent immobility (long flight, surgery)?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Sudden dyspnea with DVT risk factors raises high suspicion for PE. Go to ER immediately. Diagnosis: CT pulmonary angiography. Treatment: anticoagulation.
Gradual dyspnea over weeks may indicate heart failure, COPD, interstitial lung disease, or anemia. Workup: CXR, ECG, BNP, spirometry, CBC, pulse oximetry.
Sudden dyspnea without clear cause: PE, pneumothorax, acute asthma, anaphylaxis, arrhythmia. If severe, call 911.
Symptoms don't match a clear pattern. Schedule a visit for chest auscultation, peak flow, and CXR if needed.

nodes.question: Where is the abdominal pain?
Does the pain come in waves, worsen after fatty meals, or radiate to the right shoulder?

nodes.diagnosisBody: RUQ pain worsened by fatty food with shoulder radiation is classic for gallstones. If fever and persistent pain (>6h): acute cholecystitis. Ultrasound for diagnosis. Seek evaluation today.
Persistent RUQ pain may indicate hepatitis or liver congestion. LFTs, ultrasound, and hepatitis serology recommended.

nodes.question: Does the pain burn, worsen on empty stomach, improve with eating or antacids?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Epigastric burning improving with food suggests peptic ulcer or gastritis, often H. pylori or NSAID-related. PPI + H. pylori testing. Endoscopy if alarm symptoms.
May indicate pancreatitis (check lipase — severe if radiates to back), functional dyspepsia, or cardiac disease. If severe, urgent lipase blood test needed.

nodes.question: Did the pain start around the belly button and migrate to the lower right? Getting worse?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Classic migratory RLQ pain suggests appendicitis. Go to the ER for exam, blood tests, CT abdomen. Do not eat or drink. Surgery usually needed within 24h.
RLQ pain without migration may indicate mesenteric lymphadenitis, ovarian cyst/torsion, hernia, or Crohn's disease. Ultrasound or CT recommended.

nodes.question: Do you have changes in bowel habits (diarrhea, constipation, or alternating)?

nodes.diagnosisBody: LLQ pain with altered bowel habits: diverticulitis if acute with fever (CT + antibiotics), IBS if chronic/recurrent. Red flags needing urgent workup: blood in stool, weight loss, age >50.
May indicate renal colic (severe, colicky, radiates to groin), ovarian pathology, or constipation. Urinalysis and imaging recommended.

nodes.question: Do you have nausea, vomiting, and/or diarrhea?
Have you eaten questionable food or have others around you fallen ill?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Non-localized pain without GI symptoms: early obstruction, metabolic causes (DKA, uremia), or functional pain. If severe/persistent/distended, seek emergency evaluation.
Acute GI symptoms after contaminated food. Most cases resolve in 1-3 days. Key: oral rehydration. ER if: bloody stool, severe dehydration, high fever, symptoms >3 days.
Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea without food exposure suggest viral gastroenteritis. Self-limiting. Stay hydrated. Anti-emetics and loperamide may help.

nodes.question: Where is the pain?
Does the pain radiate down one or both legs?
Do you have numbness/weakness in legs, or difficulty with bladder/bowel control?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Non-radiating back pain is most commonly mechanical. 90% resolves within 6 weeks. Stay active, NSAIDs, heat, stretching. Red flags needing urgent imaging: trauma, weight loss, fever, progressive deficit.
Back pain with bilateral leg symptoms AND bladder/bowel dysfunction is a surgical emergency. Without urgent decompression within 24-48h, permanent paralysis may result. Go to ER immediately.
Radiating leg pain without cauda equina red flags suggests sciatica. Most improve in 6-12 weeks with NSAIDs and physical therapy. MRI if symptoms persist >6 weeks or motor deficit worsens.

nodes.question: Are the joints swollen, warm, or red?
Is only ONE joint affected?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Joint pain without swelling, worse with activity, better with rest = osteoarthritis. Treatment: weight management, exercise, paracetamol, topical NSAIDs, physiotherapy.
One hot swollen joint = gout vs septic arthritis. If fever present, joint must be aspirated urgently to exclude infection (surgical emergency). Colchicine/NSAIDs for gout; IV antibiotics for septic.
Multiple swollen joints suggest RA, psoriatic, or reactive arthritis. Blood: RF, anti-CCP, CRP, ESR. Early rheumatology referral prevents joint damage.
Muscle pain may indicate: viral illness, overexertion, fibromyalgia, statin myopathy, vitamin D deficiency, or hypothyroidism. Check CK, TSH, vitamin D, CBC.

nodes.question: Did the neck pain start after trauma (fall, accident)?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Neck pain after trauma requires cervical spine imaging to rule out fracture. Keep neck immobilized until cleared. If neurological symptoms present, go to ER.
Non-traumatic neck pain: usually posture, screens, or spondylosis. Gentle ROM exercises, heat, NSAIDs. Red flags: arm weakness, gait problems → MRI.

nodes.question: Does the rash have blisters?
Is the rash in a band-like pattern on one side of the body?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Painful unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash = herpes zoster. Start valacyclovir 1g TID within 72h. If near the eye, urgent ophthalmology referral.
Non-dermatomal blistering: contact dermatitis, pemphigoid, or drug reaction. If widespread with mucosal involvement, consider Stevens-Johnson (emergency). See dermatology.

nodes.question: Is the rash itchy?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Itchy non-blistering: eczema (flexural), contact dermatitis (localized), or urticaria (migratory wheals). Moisturizers, topical corticosteroids, oral antihistamines. See derm if persistent.
Non-itchy: psoriasis (silvery plaques), pityriasis rosea, fungal (ring-shaped), secondary syphilis (palms/soles), or vasculitis. Dermatology referral for biopsy.

nodes.question: Do you have a fever?
Has the fever lasted more than 7 days?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Fever >7 days = FUO. Broad DDx: infection (endocarditis, TB, abscess, HIV), autoimmune (lupus, Still's), malignancy (lymphoma). Blood cultures x3, CBC, CRP, LDH, ferritin, ANA, CT C/A/P, echo.
Short-duration fever is usually viral. Supportive care: rest, hydration, paracetamol. Seek care if: >39.5°C, can't keep fluids, confusion, rash, stiff neck, or immunocompromised.

nodes.question: Have you felt unusually tired or weak for more than 2 weeks?
Have you had unexplained weight loss (>5% in 6 months)?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Red flag combination. Consider: malignancy, hyperthyroidism, diabetes, chronic infection (HIV, TB), celiac disease, adrenal insufficiency. Comprehensive labs + imaging this week.

nodes.question: Do you feel sad, hopeless, or have lost interest in activities you used to enjoy?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Persistent fatigue with low mood and anhedonia may indicate major depression — common and treatable. Speak with your doctor or mental health professional. First-line: CBT and/or SSRI. Help is available.
Fatigue without mood symptoms: check for anemia (CBC, ferritin, B12), thyroid (TSH), diabetes (HbA1c), vitamin D, sleep apnea. Optimize sleep hygiene.
No clear pattern. A routine check-up with basic bloodwork (CBC, metabolic panel, thyroid, lipids), BP, and age-appropriate screenings is recommended.

nodes.question: Do you have pain or burning when urinating?
Do you also have fever, back/flank pain, or chills?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Dysuria + fever + flank pain = upper UTI (kidney infection). Needs urinalysis, culture, blood tests today. IV antibiotics if vomiting or systemically unwell.
Dysuria with frequency/urgency without systemic symptoms = uncomplicated cystitis. Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x5 days. Increase fluids. If no improvement in 48h, urine culture.

nodes.question: Do you have blood in your urine?

nodes.diagnosisBody: Visible blood in urine requires investigation. Younger: stones, UTI. Over 40: must exclude bladder cancer. Urinalysis, ultrasound, cystoscopy recommended.
Frequency, urgency, nocturia without pain/blood: BPH in men >50 (PSA + prostate exam), overactive bladder in women. Urinalysis to exclude infection. Reduce caffeine.
